stage.name: What is Blockchain? (Elementary)
stage.description: A beginner-friendly introduction to blockchain — what it is, how blocks connect, and why it matters.

elements.content: A shared notebook no one can erase — Elementary Level

Imagine you and all your friends have the exact same notebook. Every time someone writes something new, everyone's notebook automatically gets the same update. And nobody — not even a teacher — can erase what's already written!


That is a blockchain. Thousands of computers around the world all share the same digital notebook at once.

🔒 Can't Be Erased
Once written, it stays

actions.text: 🔗 What is a Blockchain?
A shared notebook no one can erase — Elementary Level

Imagine you and all your friends have the exact same notebook. Every time someone writes something new, everyone's notebook automatically gets the same update. And nobody — not even a teacher — can erase what's already written!. That is a blockchain. Thousands of computers around the world all share the same digital notebook at once
📓 Shared. Everyone has a copy
🔒 Can't Be Erased. Once written, it stays
🌍 No Boss. No one controls it
👀 Open. Anyone can read it

elements.content: Each block is like one page in the notebook
Every time a bunch of transactions happen, they get bundled into one block. That block gets a special code called a hash — like a fingerprint — then gets added to the end of the chain.
⚡ Cool Fact: If someone tried to change Block 1, its code would change — which would instantly break Block 2 and Block 3. The whole chain notices. That's why blockchains are so hard to cheat!
THE BLOCKCHAIN = ALL THE BLOCKS LINKED TOGETHER IN ORDER

actions.text: 🧱 What is a Block?
Each block is like one page in the notebook
Every time a bunch of transactions happen, they get bundled into one block. That block gets a special code called a hash — like a fingerprint — then gets added to the end of the chain
Block 1📦 Transactions. 🔑 Code: #A1B2
Block 2📦 Transactions. 🔑 Code: #C3D4
Block 3📦 Transactions. 🔑 Code: #E5F6
⚡ Cool Fact: If someone tried to change Block 1, its code would change — which would instantly break Block 2 and Block 3. The whole chain notices. That's why blockchains are so hard to cheat!
THE BLOCKCHAIN = ALL THE BLOCKS LINKED TOGETHER IN ORDER

elements.content: Before blockchain, if you wanted to send money abroad, you had to trust a bank. The bank could make mistakes, charge big fees, or even freeze your money. Blockchain lets people send money directly — no bank needed.

✗ Bank controls your money
✗ Can reject you
✗ Charges big fees
✗ Can freeze your account
✗ Takes days to send abroad

✓ You control your money
✓ Anyone can use it
✓ Tiny or zero fees
✓ Nobody can freeze it
✓ Transfers in seconds

🔗 OnlyCrypto

Runs on XRP Ledger — a blockchain that settles in 3-5 seconds with nearly zero fees. Payouts go directly to your wallet, automatically.

actions.text: 🌟 Why Does Blockchain Matter?
Old way vs New way

Before blockchain, if you wanted to send money abroad, you had to trust a bank. The bank could make mistakes, charge big fees, or even freeze your money. Blockchain lets people send money directly — no bank needed
🏦 Old Way (Bank)
⚡ New Way (Blockchain)
🔗 OnlyCryptoRuns on XRP Ledger — a blockchain that settles in 3-5 seconds with nearly zero fees. Payouts go directly to your wallet, automatically
✗ Bank controls your money. ✗ Can reject you. ✗ Charges big fees. ✗ Can freeze your account. ✗ Takes days to send abroad
✓ You control your money. ✓ Anyone can use it. ✓ Tiny or zero fees. ✓ Nobody can freeze it. ✓ Transfers in seconds

elements.content: The original record-keeping system — and why crypto improved it

A ledger is just a record book. Banks have always used ledgers to track who has what money. The problem? You have to trust the bank not to change the records.


Blockchain replaces the bank's private ledger with a public ledger that thousands of computers all hold at once. No single person controls it — so no one can cheat.

actions.text: 📓 What is a Ledger?
The original record-keeping system — and why crypto improved it

A ledger is just a record book. Banks have always used ledgers to track who has what money. The problem? You have to trust the bank not to change the records. Blockchain replaces the bank's private ledger with a public ledger that thousands of computers all hold at once. No single person controls it — so no one can cheat
📒 Traditional Ledger. One bank controls it
🌐 Blockchain Ledger. Everyone holds a copy
🔒 Immutable. Once written, it stays
✅ Trustless. No middleman needed

elements.content: The volunteers who keep the network running

A node is any computer that participates in the blockchain network. Right now, there are thousands of nodes around the world running Bitcoin and Ethereum software 24 hours a day.


Validators are special nodes that check transactions and confirm they are valid before adding them to the chain. They are the referees of the network — and they earn crypto rewards for doing it right.

🌍 Decentralized
No single point of failure

actions.text: 🖥️ Nodes & Validators
The volunteers who keep the network running

A node is any computer that participates in the blockchain network. Right now, there are thousands of nodes around the world running Bitcoin and Ethereum software 24 hours a day. Validators are special nodes that check transactions and confirm they are valid before adding them to the chain. They are the referees of the network — and they earn crypto rewards for doing it right
🖥️ Full Node. Stores entire blockchain
⚡ Validator. Checks & confirms txs
💰 Reward. Earns crypto for work
🌍 Decentralized. No single point of failure

elements.content: How individual records link together into an unbreakable chain

Each block contains a batch of transactions, a timestamp, and — crucially — a fingerprint of the previous block. This fingerprint is called a hash.


Because every block references the one before it, changing any old block would break every block that came after it. The entire network would immediately reject the fake version. That is what makes blockchain immutable — it cannot be secretly changed.

actions.text: 🔗 Blocks & Chains Explained
How individual records link together into an unbreakable chain

Each block contains a batch of transactions, a timestamp, and — crucially — a fingerprint of the previous block. This fingerprint is called a hash. Because every block references the one before it, changing any old block would break every block that came after it. The entire network would immediately reject the fake version. That is what makes blockchain immutable — it cannot be secretly changed
📦 Block. Batch of transactions
🔢 Hash. Unique fingerprint
🔗 Chain. Blocks linked in order
🚫 Immutable. Can't change the past

scenes.title: ⚖️ Proof of Work vs Proof of Stake

elements.content: ⚖️ Proof of Work vs Proof of Stake
Two ways blockchains decide who adds the next block

Proof of Work (PoW) — used by Bitcoin. Computers compete to solve a hard math puzzle. The winner adds the next block and earns a reward. Uses lots of energy.


Proof of Stake (PoS) — used by Ethereum. Validators lock up (stake) their crypto as a promise to be honest. Cheating means losing their stake. Much more energy efficient.


Both systems ensure that it is extremely expensive to attack the network.

🛡️ Security
Both are very hard to attack

actions.text: ⚖️ Proof of Work vs Proof of Stake
Two ways blockchains decide who adds the next block

Proof of Work (PoW) — used by Bitcoin. Computers compete to solve a hard math puzzle. The winner adds the next block and earns a reward. Uses lots of energy. Proof of Stake (PoS) — used by Ethereum. Validators lock up (stake) their crypto as a promise to be honest. Cheating means losing their stake. Much more energy efficient. Both systems ensure that it is extremely expensive to attack the network
⛏️ PoW. Bitcoin — energy-heavy
🪙 PoS. Ethereum — stake to validate
🌱 Energy. PoS uses 99% less energy
🛡️ Security. Both are very hard to attack

content.html:
🧱 Block Builder

Add transactions from the mempool into your block. Fill it up, then seal it to get a hash.

actions.text: Let's explore this interactively. Use the simulation below to experiment with the concepts we just covered.
Try different inputs and see how the outputs change. Hands-on exploration builds intuition faster than reading.

questions.question: What is the best way to describe a blockchain?

options.label: A shared digital notebook that thousands of computers all have a copy of
A website run by one company

questions.analysis: A blockchain is a shared digital ledger that thousands of computers all hold a copy of. No single person or company controls it — making it transparent and very hard to cheat.

questions.question: What is a 'hash' on a blockchain?

options.label: A special code (fingerprint) that identifies each block
A fee you pay to send money

questions.analysis: A hash is a unique code — like a fingerprint — assigned to each block. If anything in the block changes, the hash changes completely, making tampering obvious to the whole network.

questions.question: Why is blockchain hard to cheat?

options.label: Because it uses very strong passwords
Because changing one block breaks the codes of every block after it — the whole network notices
Because only one person can add to it

questions.analysis: Every block contains the fingerprint (hash) of the one before it. Change any block and its fingerprint changes, instantly breaking every block that comes after. The network detects this immediately.

questions.question: Which of these is an advantage of blockchain over a traditional bank?

options.label: You need a credit score to use it
Anyone can use it and transfers happen in seconds with tiny fees
A company controls all the rules

questions.analysis: Blockchain lets anyone send money to anyone in the world in seconds with very small fees — no bank account, no approval, no waiting days. That's the power of decentralized money.

actions.text: Time to test what you've learned! Answer each question and see how you do.
Take your time — the goal is understanding, not just getting the right answer.
